A fishing log app with voice-to-structured-data, external API integrations, and AI features. Built in 4 hours with zero code. Testing how much product depth AI tools can deliver at prototype speed.
Working demo in 4 hours, zero code. March 2026

import BuildTimeline from '../../components/BuildTimeline.tsx'; ## The Thesis Test catch.ai tests how much product depth AI tools can deliver at prototype speed. Not just a demo with buttons. Real product decisions: which integrations matter, how to transform messy upstream data into user value, and where AI features add depth that would normally require dedicated engineering. ## The Problem Fishing trips generate useful data. Location, conditions, what worked, what didn't. I was tracking it across four tools: handwritten notes, Google Maps pins, manually pulled USGS stream data, and ChatGPT for pattern questions. Nothing connected. I couldn't find patterns across trips, and every spot looked the same in my memory. ## The Process I brainstorm on walks, talking through product thinking with ChatGPT. That produces a guidelines file. Design and architecture decisions captured before touching any build tool. From there: ~10 prompts to set up the core structure and speech-to-text tooling. Four hours from zero to a working demo. - **Research & scoping:** ChatGPT for feature definition, API exploration, creative brainstorming - **Guidelines file:** Architecture and design decisions captured before touching Lovable - **Build:** Lovable for the full application. UI, backend, data layer. ~10 prompts to working app. - **Zero code written.** Every implementation decision was directed through prompts. ## What It Does **Voice log to AI normalization.** Talk through a trip naturally. The app transcribes and normalizes it into a structured log entry: species, location, conditions, techniques, outcome. No forms. **AI suggestions.** A fishing assistant with access to your log history. More like a knowledgeable guide than a generic chatbot. **USGS data integration** *(in progress).* Raw API responses from USGS stream gauges translated into plain-language fishing condition summaries.
## What's Actually Hard The 4-hour demo proves the thesis directionally. But the edge cases reveal where AI tooling still has friction: - **Maps integration:** Getting the UI right took more iteration than the core app logic. - **Safari microphone access:** Mobile browser permissions are a pain point that Lovable can't abstract away. - **Security:** Cloudflare configuration to prevent AI endpoint abuse required manual problem-solving outside the AI toolchain. - **USGS database:** The upstream data structure is messy. Harder to work with than expected, and not something prompts can easily fix. The pattern: core product logic is fast. Platform integrations, security, and upstream data quality are where the time goes. ## What This Proves (So Far) A product leader with AI tools can get to a working, deployed consumer app in an afternoon. But the more interesting question isn't speed to first demo. It's depth. Can you make real product decisions about external data integrations (USGS stream data into plain-language fishing conditions)? Can you build transformation layers that interpret messy upstream data? Can you ship features like voice-to-structured-data that would normally require dedicated engineering? Early answer: yes, with caveats. The core product logic and AI features are fast. Platform integrations, security, and upstream data quality are where the time goes. That's where I'm pushing now. ## Try It [catchapp.kyle-mcleod.io](https://catchapp.kyle-mcleod.io)
A manufacturing ERP built in days using Claude skills and AI workflows. Testing how domain expertise and AI-driven product processes compress the path from requirements to working system.
Solo Builder. Claude skills + ChatGPT architecture

import BuildTimeline from '../../components/BuildTimeline.tsx'; ## The Thesis Test A product leader with deep domain knowledge and the right AI workflow can compress months of requirements, architecture, and implementation into days. ERPForge tests the sharpest version of that. Can you codify the product process itself into reusable AI skills, so the next system builds faster than the last? ## The Problem Traditional ERPs exist because code was expensive. Vendors built lowest-common-denominator software for a business segment, then tried to configure it per customer. Big customers got the most features. Small ones got the base package or cobbled together Excel and point solutions. At MacroFab, we built manufacturing workflows in-house because MRPs were expensive and slow to implement. The cost structure forced that tradeoff. Build it yourself or settle for software that doesn't match your operations. What if the cost structure changed? ## The Approach I architected the system with ChatGPT. Talked through the design on a walk, generated a `CLAUDE.md` file and an architecture doc, then used Claude to build the full platform in a day. The interesting part isn't the ERP. It's the layer on top: Claude skills for design review, discovery question templates, documentation generation. The skills *are* the product. The ERP is proof the process works. - **`/design-review`** structured 10-section review before writing any code - **Discovery templates** 80 questions across 12 categories for BOM and product scoping - **`/write-docs`** auto-generates API reference, migration guides, and changelogs The BOM rebuild is the clearest example. I gave Claude context on typical BOM structures and manufacturing processes, gave it skills around BOM information, and had it interview me about product workflows and configurations. Then it rebuilt the data model. Four hours from kickoff to a restructured, production-ready BOM system. Work that would normally take a systems integrator weeks. ## What It Does Eight integrated modules: Products & BOM, Quotes, Orders, Inventory, Purchasing, Manufacturing, Shipping, and Invoices. The full quote-to-order-to-ship workflow runs end to end. The BOM system is the deepest module. Recursive multi-level BOMs with revision control, alternate components, CSV import with column auto-detection, and depth-first cost rollup through the full tree.
## What's Actually Hard Cloud hosting was new territory for me. Took a full day to get Railway + Cloudflare configured with containers, DNS, and security. The next challenge: turning manual skills into automated agent workflows. Right now, Claude follows the skills when I prompt it. The goal is a system that runs the full interview, build, and iterate loop without manual orchestration. That's the difference between "I can build an ERP fast" and "anyone with domain knowledge can." ## What This Proves (So Far) A product leader with the right AI workflow can stand up a realistic manufacturing ERP in days. But the bigger bet isn't one ERP. It's whether the skills and templates that built this one can build the next one faster, and eventually build themselves. ## Try It [erpforge.kyle-mcleod.io](https://erpforge.kyle-mcleod.io)
AI development tools are the new leverage. They won't topple every software moat, but they change who gets to attack.

import BarbarianComparison from "../../components/BarbarianComparison";
import LeverageShift from "../../components/LeverageShift";

## The Original Barbarians

In 1988, KKR bought RJR Nabisco for $25 billion. They didn't know how to run a tobacco-and-food conglomerate. They didn't need to.

What made the deal possible wasn't operational expertise. It was a new class of financial instrument. Michael Milken's junk bond network gave firms like KKR access to capital structures that turned $15 million in equity into multi-billion-dollar acquisitions. The leverage ratio was absurd. The playbook was simple: find an undervalued company, load it with debt, extract value.

Most of those firms weren't better operators. Many failed. But enough succeeded to permanently change how corporate America thought about defensibility. The moat wasn't ownership anymore. It was whether you could actually run the business once you had it.

  ]}
/>

## The New Leverage

AI development tools (Lovable, Cursor, Claude, v0) are doing to software what junk bonds did to acquisitions. They're not making everyone a software engineer. They're giving people who understand business logic the ability to build without one.

That distinction matters. The bottleneck in most SaaS platforms was never the individual transaction logic. It's straightforward. The complexity is in encoding thousands of business rules, edge cases, and operational workflows into code. That required teams of engineers who often didn't fully understand the domain, translating requirements from people who did.

Now the people who understand the business logic can write the rules themselves. Not all of them. Not perfectly. But enough to get 70% of the way to a working platform in days instead of months.

And that changes the math on whether to attempt the other 30%.

## What Still Holds

Distribution. Sales teams. Network effects. Regulatory compliance. Data moats built on years of customer activity. DevOps at scale. None of these get easier because someone can prompt their way to a working prototype.

The barbarians of the 1980s still had to operate the companies they bought. Many couldn't. Building the platform is becoming the easy part. Running it, selling it, and scaling it still require everything they always did.

## The Shift

KKR didn't win every deal. They didn't need to. They changed the game by making it possible to *try*. Incumbents who had coasted on the sheer difficulty of replication suddenly had to defend on merit.

AI tools won't topple every SaaS company. But they're about to increase the number of credible attackers, lower the cost of each attempt, and expose which systems were only defensible because they were expensive to rebuild.
